stories.headline: Colorado Legislature Sends $48.6 Billion State Budget to Governor After Medicaid Cuts
stories.summary:
The Colorado legislature finalized a $48.6 billion spending plan Tuesday, sending it to Gov. Jared Polis after a final round of adjustments aimed at closing a roughly $1.5 billion budget shortfall. Medicaid bore the brunt of the reductions, with the Joint Budget Committee making targeted cuts to address a gap between available revenues and the cost of continuing current government services.

A central flashpoint was the Cover All Coloradans program, which provides healthcare to children and pregnant women regardless of immigration status and has cost six times more than projected this fiscal year. The JBC moved forward with enrollment caps and limits on dental benefits, rejecting a House proposal to redirect prison funds to preserve the program at higher levels.

Among the more consequential additions was $7.7 million to allow children with disabilities receiving 24/7 medical care to automatically continue that coverage as adults through January 2027, avoiding a forced transition to a waitlist projected to grow to 14 years. Maternal and neonatal caregivers were also exempted from a Medicaid reimbursement reduction.

The budget takes effect July 1 and reflects difficult tradeoffs that left some lawmakers emotional. 'The nature of our budget is horrible. We are forced to make some of the worst decisions that I have ever had to make,' said state Rep. Kyle Brown, a Louisville Democrat on the JBC.

stories.headline: Colorado Lawmakers Introduce Sweeping Security Overhaul for Elected Officials and Judges
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators unveiled a 60-page bill this week that would dramatically expand protections for elected officials, legislative staff, and judicial employees, citing a rise in political violence nationwide. House Bill 1422, sponsored by Rep. Chad Clifford, Senate President James Coleman, and Sen. Lisa Frizell, contains nearly 30 sections and was introduced without a fiscal note, leaving its cost to the state unknown.

Key provisions include the creation of an Administrator of Legislative Safety — a law enforcement officer serving as a point of contact for General Assembly members — and an expansion of individuals protected by the Colorado State Patrol at the Capitol. The bill also establishes a courthouse security task force and requires sheriffs to adopt its recommended standards.

Additional measures would allow judicial employees, elected officials, and their staff to request removal of personal information from online public records, with civil remedies available if those requests are not honored. The bill also repeals a requirement that candidate disclosure statements and property descriptions be publicly available on the Secretary of State's website.

Colorado lawmakers said they began prioritizing legislative security after Minnesota House Speaker Melissa Hortman and her husband were killed last June, and following a series of other high-profile attacks on public officials across the country.

stories.headline: Colorado Lawmakers Push New Conversion Therapy Bill After Supreme Court Ruling
stories.summary:
Following a U.S. Supreme Court ruling last month that called Colorado's existing conversion therapy ban potentially unconstitutional on free speech grounds, a group of Democratic state lawmakers is advancing House Bill 26-1322, a revised approach that would classify conversion therapy as harmful professional conduct rather than prohibited speech. The bill would allow victims to sue licensed mental health providers for damages, with no statute of limitations.

The Supreme Court sided with a Colorado Springs Christian counselor who argued the current ban violated her First Amendment rights by restricting her ability to discuss conversion therapy with clients. Rather than overturning the ban outright, the court remanded the case to a lower court, creating a legal window for the legislature to act.

Sponsor Rep. Karen McCormick of Longmont said the bill is intended to deter licensed therapists from resuming the practice. 'The purpose of this bill is seriously to send a chilling effect to any licensed professional therapist who may think about bringing that practice back,' she said. The measure would not apply to religious leaders.

Every major U.S. medical organization, including the American Psychological Association, has discredited conversion therapy, linking it to increased anxiety, depression, and suicide risk. Colorado's original ban has been in place since 2019.

stories.headline: Colorado Higher Education Funding Formula Rewrite Targets Equity Among Institutions
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers are advancing a rewrite of the state's higher education funding formula in an effort to more equitably distribute state dollars among colleges and universities. Colorado Mesa University President John Marshall said the new formula could address longstanding disparities in per-student state funding across institutions.

Metropolitan State University of Denver President Janine Davidson said the proposed updates better account for part-time students and other non-traditional enrollment patterns, which have historically been undercounted in funding calculations. MSU Denver, which serves a large population of working adults and first-generation college students, stands to benefit from such adjustments.

The reform reflects broader pressure on the state's higher education system as budget shortfalls force difficult choices about how limited public dollars are allocated. Lawmakers have described the formula update as a way to better align funding with the actual students being served rather than historical enrollment benchmarks.

stories.headline: Federal Appeals Court Dismisses Lawsuit from Men Wrongly Arrested in Downtown Denver Shooting
stories.summary:
The U.S. Court of Appeals for the 10th Circuit has dismissed a lawsuit brought by two men who were arrested as suspects in a 2021 downtown Denver shooting despite allegedly being the victims. Trevor Puller and Deandre Hutchinson say they were driving in Lower Downtown when an unidentified man fired at their car, prompting them to pull into an alley where a nearby officer blocked them and placed them in handcuffs.

Prosecutors charged the pair with 19 counts of attempted murder, but the district attorney's office later dropped all charges based on HALO surveillance footage, citing 'strong video evidence' but no reasonable likelihood of conviction. Puller and Hutchinson subsequently sued Officers Nicholas Greco and Nicodemus Werth, alleging the officers fabricated probable cause statements to send 'two young men they falsely labeled as gang members to prison for the rest of their lives.'

A federal district court dismissed the case in December 2024, ruling the plaintiffs had not identified a prior case establishing that similar police conduct was unconstitutional. The 10th Circuit panel upheld that decision, with the city arguing that the available evidence at the time justified the arrests as a prudent response to what appeared to be a drive-by shooting.

The dismissal rested on qualified immunity, the judicially created doctrine that shields government officials from civil liability unless they clearly violate established legal rights. The ruling is the latest example of qualified immunity blocking civil claims against law enforcement in Colorado courts.

stories.headline: Man Killed, Woman Injured in Single-Vehicle Crash on C-470 in Jefferson County
stories.summary:
A 31-year-old man was killed and a 30-year-old woman was injured Tuesday morning when their red Toyota SUV drifted off westbound C-470 on the bridge over Ken Caryl Avenue in Jefferson County and struck a guardrail. The crash occurred at approximately 9:19 a.m., according to the Colorado State Patrol.

A 2-year-old boy in the vehicle was uninjured. All three were transported to a hospital, where the man was pronounced dead. The woman sustained non-life-threatening injuries. The group was traveling back to New Mexico at the time.

Westbound C-470 was closed at the West Ken Caryl Avenue exit for approximately two hours before reopening around 11:30 a.m. The Colorado State Patrol's vehicular crimes unit is investigating, and the man's name is to be released by the Jefferson County Coroner's Office.

stories.headline: Sheridan Teachers Strike Ends After Board Ratifies New Agreement with Educators Association
stories.summary:
The Sheridan Board of Education voted unanimously to ratify a new contract with the Sheridan Educators Association, officially ending a strike that had lasted more than three weeks. The agreement resolves a labor dispute that had disrupted classes for students in the small district south of Denver.

Details of the contract terms, including wage increases or other provisions that led to the resolution, were not immediately disclosed. The unanimous board vote signals broad consensus among school officials to restore normal operations. The strike's end comes after weeks of negotiation that drew attention to compensation and working conditions in one of the metro area's smaller school districts.

stories.headline: East Colfax Bus Rapid Transit Project Reaches Halfway Point as Construction Disruptions Continue
stories.summary:
Denver's East Colfax Bus Rapid Transit project has reached its halfway point, with crews installing the first station arch in the historic corridor at Quebec Street on Monday night. The Denver Department of Transportation and Infrastructure marked the milestone with a community event at Fiction Beer Company, aimed at encouraging residents and visitors to continue patronizing businesses along the construction zone.

The project, which began in late 2024, will create dedicated bus lanes from Broadway to Yosemite Street, with buses operating in mixed traffic from Yosemite to I-225. When complete, DOTI expects the BRT to reduce travel times along the corridor by 15 to 30 minutes, with buses arriving every 4.3 minutes during peak hours.

Local businesses have reported significant disruptions since construction began, including difficulty for customers accessing parking lots during utility installation phases. Antique vendor Park Hill Treasures said conditions have improved but remain challenging, while expressing optimism that the finished project could bring new foot traffic to the area.

Project director Jonathan Stewart said the transformation is necessary as Denver grows and street widening is not a viable option. 'What we can do is we can make the buses move faster, make it more reliable, and make it cheaper,' he said.

stories.headline: Denver Weighing Whether to Allow Bars and Nightclubs to Stay Open Until 4 a.m.
stories.summary:
Denver's Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection is gauging public interest in a proposal to extend bar and nightclub hours from the current 2 a.m. closing time to 4 a.m. The idea has not yet been formally presented to the Denver City Council.

If the proposal advances and is approved, the new hours would not take effect until January, following an expected end-of-June passage date. The change would represent a significant shift in Denver's nightlife landscape and could have implications for public safety, noise, and transportation planning in entertainment districts.

No formal public hearing date has been announced, and community feedback is still being gathered before the measure moves forward in the city's legislative process.

stories.headline: Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance Names Former DIA Executive as Permanent CEO
stories.summary:
The Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance has named a former Denver International Airport executive as its permanent chief executive officer, following the individual's service as acting CEO for the past several months. The appointment formalizes a leadership transition at one of Denver's most prominent cultural institutions.

Cristal Torres DeHerrera's background in managing large public-facing operations at DIA positions her to oversee the zoo's conservation programs, visitor experience, and ongoing capital projects. The Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance manages both the Denver Zoo and broader wildlife conservation efforts.

The organization serves hundreds of thousands of visitors annually and plays a significant role in regional conservation initiatives. Further details on strategic priorities under the new CEO were not immediately available.

stories.headline: Colorado Faces Above-Average Rain This Week After Dry Start to 2026
stories.summary:
A multi-day storm system moving through Colorado this week could bring some areas more rainfall in a few days than Denver has received all year. Denver has recorded just 1.52 inches of precipitation so far in 2026, according to National Weather Service data, leaving the state in drought conditions with elevated fire danger heading into spring.

Forecasters expect cool, unsettled conditions to persist through at least Thursday, with more widespread rain arriving midweek. Snow is expected in the foothills and along the Palmer Divide on Thursday and Friday, with the possibility of a few snowflakes in the Denver metro overnight, though no accumulation is expected at lower elevations.

Warmer and drier weather is forecast to return by the weekend, with highs climbing back into the upper 60s Saturday and lower 70s Sunday. The precipitation, while welcome given drought conditions, comes with the typical disruptions of a late-spring Colorado storm system.

stories.headline: Nuggets Force Game 6 Against Timberwolves, Trail Series 3-2 After Game 5 Win
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets kept their playoff hopes alive with a 125-113 victory over the Minnesota Timberwolves in Game 5 Monday night at Ball Arena, reducing their first-round series deficit to 3-2. The win came as a relieved home crowd, which serenaded Timberwolves forward Jaden McDaniels with sustained boos throughout the night, gave the team a boost following a string of difficult losses.

Nikola Jokic made history during the third quarter, recording his 10th rebound to tie former teammate Russell Westbrook for the most career triple-doubles when combining regular-season and postseason statistics. The milestone came on a night when Jokic and his teammates delivered the kind of performance that has defined Denver's recent playoff runs.

Forward Aaron Gordon was unavailable for the game, watching from the bench in street clothes — a continuing concern for the Nuggets as they try to extend the series. Game 6 is scheduled for Thursday in Minnesota, where Denver will need a win to force a decisive seventh game.

joke.joke: I was at a restaurant and the waiter asked, 'Would you like a booth or a table?' I said, 'What's the difference?' He said, 'A booth has cushions.' I said, 'Booth, forever booth.'

topStory.headline: Federal Judge Dismisses DOJ Lawsuit Seeking Arizona Voter Registration Data
topStory.summary:
A federal judge on Tuesday dismissed a U.S. Department of Justice lawsuit against Arizona that sought access to the state's detailed voter registration records, dealing the Trump administration its sixth legal defeat in a nationwide campaign to obtain voter data from states. U.S. District Judge Susan Brnovich, a Trump appointee, ruled that Title III of the Civil Rights Act of 1960 does not grant the Attorney General the authority to demand Arizona's statewide voter registration list. The judge dismissed the case with prejudice, writing that "amendment would be legally futile."

Arizona Secretary of State Adrian Fontes, a Democrat, had refused to comply with the Justice Department's request, which sought voters' full names, dates of birth, home addresses, driver's license numbers and partial Social Security numbers. Fontes and Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes issued a joint statement calling the ruling a vindication, saying the data "contains the sensitive personal information of millions of Arizona voters" and accusing the federal government of overreach. The Justice Department did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

The DOJ sued Fontes in January after he declined to hand over the data last summer. Federal officials have maintained that access to detailed voter records is necessary to verify that states are complying with the National Voter Registration Act and the Help America Vote Act. In a separate case involving Rhode Island, a Justice Department attorney acknowledged that the government intended to run voter registration data through a Department of Homeland Security database to check the citizenship status of registered voters.

Courts have now rejected similar DOJ efforts in Arizona, Rhode Island, California, Massachusetts, Michigan and Oregon. In Georgia, a judge dismissed a DOJ lawsuit on procedural grounds, and the government has refiled that case. At least 13 states, including Texas, Ohio and Indiana, have either provided or agreed to provide their voter registration data to the department. The string of losses raises significant legal questions about the scope of federal authority over state-administered election records.

The broader campaign reflects President Trump's sustained push to expand the federal government's role in election administration, with the administration frequently citing concerns about noncitizen voting, which election officials and researchers describe as rare. Critics, including Democratic and some Republican state officials, have argued the data requests violate state and federal privacy laws. It remains unclear whether the Justice Department will appeal the Arizona ruling or adjust its legal strategy in the remaining pending cases.
